# Bundle Hot-Spots Findings — 2026-04-07

Output of Probe B5 from the cleanup-and-perf-investigation backlog (`2026-04-06-perf-backlog.md`). This is a deep-dive into the bundle analyzer treemap to identify what is **actually** making the heaviest routes heavy, so the follow-up perf execution spec has concrete targets instead of guesses.

**Method:** parsed `window.chartData` JSON embedded in `.next/analyze/client.html` (generated by `ANALYZE=true bun run build`), correlated chunk hashes with route assignments via `.next/app-build-manifest.json`, and traced the static import chains for the worst leaks.

**Branch:** `chore/perf-inspection-b5` (off `main` at commit `66588a3` — post-cleanup-merge)

**Build context:** all numbers are post-cleanup, post-tailwind-merge, post-PR-#11.

---

## TL;DR — biggest finding

**🚨 The `/[communitySlug]/about` page leaks ~1 MB of video player runtime (510 KB `mux-player-react` + 500 KB `hls.js`) on every visit, because `PageBuilder.tsx` statically imports `VideoSection.tsx` which statically imports `MuxPlayerComponent`.** No conditional loading. Every visitor of every community's about page downloads the full Mux + HLS stack regardless of whether the community has a video section. This is a **bug**, not a refactoring opportunity.

**Single-line fix** (rough sketch — actual implementation goes in the next round):

```ts
// components/PageBuilder.tsx
- import VideoSection from "./sections/VideoSection";
+ const VideoSection = dynamic(() => import("./sections/VideoSection"), { ssr: false });
```

That one change alone saves ~280 KB gzipped on the about page (613 kB → ~330 kB First Load JS, projected). Possibly the highest impact-to-effort win in the entire codebase.

---

## Top 10 chunks by parsed size

Sorted by parsed JS size (ungzipped). Numbers are what the V8 parser sees, not what hits the wire — gzip column is the wire size.

| # | Chunk | Parsed | Gzip | Contents |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | `873-9fd3...js` | **510 KB** | 132 KB | `@mux/mux-player-react/dist/index.mjs` (one big concat) |
| 2 | `a4634e51-99c...js` | **500 KB** | 152 KB | `hls.js/dist/hls.mjs` (transitive dep of mux-player) |
| 3 | `144d3bae.067...js` | **420 KB** | 106 KB | `livekit-client/dist/livekit-client.esm.mjs` |
| 4 | `5668-c742...js` | **206 KB** | 62 KB | `@tiptap` (152 KB) + `@dnd-kit` (130 KB) + `prosemirror-model` (126 KB) |
| 5 | `fd9d1056-1e8...js` | 169 KB | 52 KB | `react-dom.production.min.js` (universal) |
| 6 | `app/[communitySlug]/page-684...js` | 166 KB | 38 KB | The community landing page's own code (very large for a page chunk) |
| 7 | `framework-8e0...js` | 137 KB | 44 KB | Next.js framework (universal) |
| 8 | `4049-d1a4...js` | **132 KB** | 41 KB | `nextstepjs/dist/NextStepReact.js` (262 modules) |
| 9 | `2117-5f5a...js` | 121 KB | 31 KB | `next/dist` internals (universal) |
| 10 | `665.ac95...js` | 119 KB | 37 KB | `@livekit/components-react/dist` |

**Universal load (every route):** chunks 5, 7, 9, plus webpack runtime, plus app-router runtime ≈ **88 kB First Load JS** (matches the build output's "First Load JS shared by all routes").

---

## Per-route hot-spots — heaviest 5 pages

For each, the route's First Load JS in the build output, the heavy chunks it specifically loads (beyond the universal kernel), and a one-line diagnosis.

### 1. `/[communitySlug]/about` — **613 kB First Load JS** 🚨 LEAK

**Heavy chunks loaded:**
| Chunk | Gzip | Why on this page |
|---|---|---|
| `873-` (mux-player-react) | 132 KB | 🚨 LEAK — about pages don't show video |
| `a4634e51-` (hls.js) | 152 KB | 🚨 LEAK — same chain as Mux |
| `5668-` (TipTap + dnd-kit) | 62 KB | PageBuilder uses TipTap for the description editor + dnd-kit for the section reordering UI |
| `70e0d97a-` (prosemirror-view) | 30 KB | TipTap runtime |

**Total wasted on the leak: ~284 KB gzipped (almost half of First Load JS)**

**Root cause** (verified by tracing imports):
```
app/[communitySlug]/about/page.tsx
  └─ import PageBuilder from "@/components/PageBuilder"        // line 8
        └─ import VideoSection from "./sections/VideoSection"   // PageBuilder line 22
              └─ import MuxPlayerComponent from "@mux/mux-player-react"  // VideoSection top
                    └─ (transitively bundles hls.js)
```

The import is **unconditional**. Even communities with zero VideoSection blocks ship the entire video stack.

**Fixes (in order of effort):**
1. **Lazy-load `VideoSection`** in `PageBuilder.tsx` via `next/dynamic({ssr: false})` — 1-line change. Saves ~284 KB gzipped on about page. Also unblocks fix #2 below.
2. **Lazy-load TipTap-driven `TextSection`** the same way — saves another ~30 KB gzipped on about page when the community has no text sections.
3. **Architectural** (next round): Split the about page into a server shell that fetches `about_page.sections` from DB and renders the static HTML server-side, with client islands ONLY for the section types that need interactivity (video player, image gallery). Visitors-mode would never load `PageBuilder` itself at all — only admins editing the page would.

**Realistic target after fixes 1+2: ~320 kB First Load JS** (down from 613 kB, ~48% reduction).

---

### 2. `/[communitySlug]/classroom/[courseSlug]` — **611 kB First Load JS**

**Heavy chunks loaded:**
| Chunk | Gzip | Why on this page |
|---|---|---|
| `873-` (mux-player-react) | 132 KB | ✅ Justified — actual lesson video player |
| `a4634e51-` (hls.js) | 152 KB | ✅ Justified — needed by Mux |
| `5668-` (TipTap + dnd-kit) | 62 KB | ⚠️ Likely from PageBuilder import chain again |
| `70e0d97a-` (prosemirror-view) | 30 KB | TipTap runtime |
| `6529-` | 16 KB | (unidentified, small) |

**Diagnosis:** the Mux + HLS load is justified here because the page IS the video player. The TipTap + dnd-kit + prosemirror combo is the same leak as the about page — the lesson page probably renders course descriptions which use TipTap, but unconditionally imports the editor instead of just the read-only renderer.

**Fixes:**
1. **Read-only TipTap rendering** — TipTap supports `editable: false` which doesn't need the full editor toolbar/state machine. If lesson descriptions are rendered (not edited) on this page, switch to a lightweight HTML renderer instead of mounting the full editor.
2. **Lazy-load TipTap** behind `'edit description'` interaction (admin-only) — same `next/dynamic` pattern.
3. **(Architectural)** Split into server shell (course metadata + lesson list from DB) + client island (the actual MuxPlayer for the active lesson). MuxPlayer stays client because it needs WebRTC/HLS init.

**Realistic target: ~480 kB First Load JS** (the video player floor is ~430 kB and can't be reduced — that's a known cost of feature-rich video).

---

### 3. `/[communitySlug]` — **411 kB First Load JS** (highest-traffic page)

**Heavy chunks loaded:**
| Chunk | Gzip | Why on this page |
|---|---|---|
| `5668-` (TipTap + dnd-kit) | 62 KB | Post composer uses TipTap |
| `70e0d97a-` (prosemirror-view) | 30 KB | TipTap runtime |
| `4049-` (nextstepjs) | **41 KB** | ⚠️ Onboarding tour library — only triggers for first-time users, but loads for everyone |
| `4163-` | 17 KB | (small, page-specific) |
| `5227-` | (small) | (page-specific) |
| `app/[communitySlug]/page-...` | **38 KB** | The page chunk itself is unusually large (166 KB parsed) for one page |

**Diagnosis:** No video runtime. Main weight is TipTap (composer) + NextStepJS (tour) + the page's own code (which is huge — 166 KB parsed for one page is a smell). This is the highest-traffic page in the app, so even small wins have big absolute impact.

**Fixes:**
1. **Lazy-load NextStepJS** behind first-time-user check. The current `NextStepWrapper` statically imports `NextStep from "nextstepjs"`. Wrap it with `next/dynamic({ssr: false})` so the chunk loads only when the user actually has an unfinished tour. Saves 41 KB gzipped.
2. **Lazy-load `ComposerBox` TipTap editor** behind the focus/click interaction. The composer is at the bottom of the feed; users scroll past it. Most page loads don't activate the composer. Saves ~60 KB gzipped.
3. **Investigate why `page-...js` is 166 KB parsed for a single page chunk.** That's a lot. Likely candidates: too many inline modal components, too many `useState`/`useEffect` blocks (43 hooks were counted in this file in Probe A). Refactoring to pull modals into separately code-split components could save another 30-50 KB.
4. **(Architectural)** Server-fetch community data + thread feed in an RSC shell. The 43 hooks → smaller hooks-only client islands.

**Realistic target: ~250 kB First Load JS** (down from 411 kB, ~40% reduction).

---

### 4. `/[communitySlug]/private-lessons` — **218 kB First Load JS**

**Heavy chunks beyond universal:** none of the big offenders. Just shared chunks + page chunk + a small `4163-`.

**Diagnosis:** This page is in reasonable shape. The First Load JS is dominated by the universal 88 kB + the page's own code (~58 KB). No leaks. Page-level RSC conversion would still help (some of the static lesson list could be server-rendered) but the absolute win would be modest (~30-50 kB).

**Priority:** **Low.** Not worth dedicated work in the next round.

---

### 5. `/[communitySlug]/classroom` — **200 kB First Load JS**

**Heavy chunks beyond universal:** `3145-`, `6529-` (both small). No big offenders.

**Diagnosis:** Course grid page. Already lean. Same situation as private-lessons — RSC conversion would shave ~30-50 kB but not transformative.

**Priority:** **Low.** Bundle optimization is not the main issue here.

---

## Cross-route observations

### Observation 1: NextStepJS bloats the highest-traffic page

`nextstepjs` is a 41 KB gzipped onboarding tour library. It's statically imported by `components/NextStepWrapper.tsx`, which is mounted in `app/layout.tsx`. **However**, the build manifest shows `chunk 4049-` is only loaded by `/[communitySlug]/page` — Next.js's per-route code splitting is correctly excluding it from other routes.

So NextStepJS is NOT a universal hit, but it IS hitting the single highest-traffic route in the app. That's still the wrong tradeoff: the tour fires for first-time users only (maybe 1-5% of sessions on a mature community), but 100% of community page loads pay the 41 kB cost.

**Fix:** make `NextStepWrapper` use `next/dynamic` to lazy-load the underlying `NextStep` component. The wrapper itself stays in the layout (so the API surface is unchanged), but the heavy module only loads when the wrapper is actually needed.

### Observation 2: PageBuilder is the worst-offender component

`PageBuilder.tsx` is statically imported by both `/[communitySlug]/about/page.tsx` and (probably) `/[communitySlug]/classroom/[courseSlug]/page.tsx`. PageBuilder unconditionally imports all section types (`HeroSection`, `TextSection`, `ImageSection`, `CTASection`, `VideoSection`). This means **every consumer of PageBuilder ships every section's runtime**, regardless of which sections that particular community actually uses.

**Fix:** convert PageBuilder's section imports to a `next/dynamic` map keyed by section type. Only sections that appear in the loaded `about_page.sections` array get their JS shipped.

```ts
// Pattern (sketch — actual implementation in next round)
const sectionComponents = {
  hero: dynamic(() => import("./sections/HeroSection"), { ssr: false }),
  text: dynamic(() => import("./sections/TextSection"), { ssr: false }),
  video: dynamic(() => import("./sections/VideoSection"), { ssr: false }),
  image: dynamic(() => import("./sections/ImageSection"), { ssr: false }),
  cta: dynamic(() => import("./sections/CTASection"), { ssr: false }),
};
```

This single refactor fixes the about page leak AND reduces every PageBuilder consumer's bundle proportionally.

### Observation 3: TipTap is everywhere it shouldn't be

TipTap is the active rich-text editor. It's used by:
- `Editor.tsx` (full editor)
- `Thread.tsx` (post composer)
- `TextSection.tsx` (PageBuilder text block)
- Probably the lesson description editor in `classroom/[courseSlug]`
- Probably the about page description block in `PageBuilder`

For **editing**, TipTap's full runtime is necessary. For **rendering/displaying** rich text content, it isn't — the saved content is HTML, and a simple `dangerouslySetInnerHTML` (with sanitization) is all you need to display it.

**Fix pattern:** introduce a lightweight `<RichTextRenderer html={html} />` component for read-only display. Switch all "view" modes to use it. Keep TipTap only for "edit" modes and lazy-load it.

### Observation 4: livekit-client lazy-loads correctly already ✅

`livekit-client.esm.mjs` is 420 KB / 106 KB gzipped. It's NOT in any of the heavy non-video routes — only loads when an actual live class room or private lesson video session is active. The Stream-Hub integration done earlier today already uses dynamic imports for this, which is great.

### Observation 5: Universal kernel is well-bounded

The 88 KB First Load JS shared across all routes is React DOM (52 KB) + Next.js framework (44 KB) + a tiny runtime/app-router kernel. There's nothing to trim there without major framework work. **This is the floor.**

---

## Concrete fix priorities for the next round

Sorted by **impact-per-effort** (gzip-saved-per-line-of-code-changed). All are concrete enough to estimate.

| # | Fix | Effort | Saves (gzip) | Affected routes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **1** | **`PageBuilder.tsx`: dynamic-import all 5 section components** | S (10 lines) | **~284 KB** | `/about` (huge), `/classroom/[courseSlug]` (medium) |
| 2 | `NextStepWrapper.tsx`: dynamic-import the underlying NextStep | XS (3 lines) | **41 KB** | `/[communitySlug]` |
| 3 | `Thread.tsx` / `ComposerBox.tsx`: lazy-load TipTap behind composer focus | S (10 lines) | ~60 KB | `/[communitySlug]` |
| 4 | Introduce `<RichTextRenderer />` for read-only display, switch viewers to it | M (~30 lines + N call sites) | ~30 KB per affected route | All routes that DISPLAY (not edit) rich text |
| 5 | Investigate the 166-KB `[communitySlug]/page` chunk: split modals out, code-split heavy state machines | M-L | ~30-50 KB | `/[communitySlug]` |
| 6 | Architectural: convert `/about` to RSC shell + client island | L | Marginal extra after fix #1 | `/about` |
| 7 | Architectural: convert `/[communitySlug]` to RSC shell + client island | L | Marginal extra after fix #2-5 | `/[communitySlug]` |

**Key insight:** fixes 1-3 are **mechanical** (one or two-line changes per file). They are *almost trivial enough for Phase 5 of the cleanup round* but I deliberately stopped before doing them because they touch real production code paths and need testing. They'd be the first 3 commits of a new dedicated round.

The architectural RSC splits (fixes 6-7) are the biggest LOC changes but produce **smaller incremental wins** AFTER fixes 1-5 are done. The reason: RSC splits move code from the client bundle to the server, but they don't eliminate the code. Fixes 1-5 actually eliminate the code entirely (or load it conditionally). Combined: do fixes 1-5 first, measure, then decide if RSC splits are still worth the effort.

---

## What this means for the follow-up perf execution spec

**Old plan (perf-backlog Top 5):** A4/A5/A6 — three giant page-level RSC conversions. Estimated: multi-day each. Total scope: 3-5 dev days.

**New plan (after this analysis):** start with the 3 mechanical fixes above, ship them as their own small round, **measure**, then decide if the full RSC conversion is still needed. Most of the projected wins come from fixes 1-3, which are an afternoon of work.

Recommended next-round shape:

- **Round A (small, 1-2 days):** "Lazy-load the leaky imports." 3 fixes, 3 commits, before/after measurements. Probably ships ~250-350 kB of gzip savings on the heaviest routes. Same workflow as cleanup: spec → plan → implement → measure.

- **Round B (larger, 3-5 days, only if Round A's measurements suggest it's still worth it):** "RSC conversion for the heaviest pages." Architectural refactor of `/about`, `/[communitySlug]`, and possibly `/classroom/[courseSlug]`. Bigger risk surface, smaller incremental wins.

This is a much better shape than committing to a 3-5 day architectural refactor before knowing if the simpler fixes get us most of the way there.

---

## Methodology / reproducibility

To reproduce these numbers from a fresh clone:

```bash
cd /home/debian/apps/dance-hub-preprod
git checkout chore/perf-inspection-b5  # or main if this is merged
rm -rf .next
ANALYZE=true bun run build
# Reports land in .next/analyze/{client,nodejs,edge}.html
```

Then:
- Open `.next/analyze/client.html` in a browser to see the interactive treemap
- The chunk → modules data is also embedded as JSON in `window.chartData` inside the HTML — extractable with a small Python script (used here)
- Route → chunks mapping is in `.next/app-build-manifest.json`

The `.next/analyze/*.html` files are intentionally NOT committed (they're large and rebuildable).

---

## Files referenced in this report (for the next round)

Items that need to be modified by Round A:

- `components/PageBuilder.tsx` — convert section imports to `next/dynamic`
- `components/NextStepWrapper.tsx` — convert NextStep import to `next/dynamic`
- `components/Thread.tsx` and/or `components/community/ComposerBox.tsx` — lazy-load TipTap behind composer focus
- (For fix #4): introduce `components/RichTextRenderer.tsx` (new file) and audit display sites

Items that need investigation by Round A's planning phase:
- `app/[communitySlug]/page.tsx` — the 166 KB page chunk needs a "what's in here" audit
- `components/sections/VideoSection.tsx` — verify the dynamic-import doesn't break the editor's drag-and-drop preview

---

## Open questions for Round A's brainstorming

1. Does `PageBuilder.tsx` ever render in `'edit'` mode for non-admins? If only admins ever edit pages, the entire `PageBuilder` itself can be dynamic-imported behind an `isAdmin` check and visitors get a much lighter `<PageRenderer>` instead.

2. The about page already has `'use client'` at line 1. Does it need to stay client-side at all? If `about_page.sections` is fetched from the DB (which it is), there's no reason the page can't be a server component that renders the saved JSON directly.

3. For the lesson description on `/classroom/[courseSlug]`: is it ever edited inline, or only edited on a separate `/admin/courses/[courseId]/edit` page? If the latter, the lesson page only needs read-only rendering — no TipTap at all.

4. NextStepJS: how often does the tour actually fire for users in production? If it's <5%, the lazy-load fix is a clear win. If it's higher (e.g., it's a recurring tour that fires on multiple events), we might want the chunk to preload eagerly anyway.

These questions are the input to the brainstorming phase of the next round.
